fund.name: UTI Nifty Next 50 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: The Nifty Next 50 is the index of companies ranked 51–100 by market size — these are tomorrow's blue-chips today. At only 0.20% yearly fee, this is the core of the portfolio. UTI is the largest and oldest manager of this index fund with ₹4,725 Cr in assets, giving you operational stability. 3-year CAGR of 21.4% at near-zero cost — no active manager has consistently beaten this after fees. This is your largest holding because it gives you maximum growth with minimum cost.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A passive (index-tracking) fund covering the top 150 mid-sized companies in India — businesses that are already established but still have serious growth runway. 5-year CAGR of 18.7% at just 0.20% yearly fee. No fund manager risk, pure market exposure. Motilal Oswal runs ₹2,450 Cr in this fund. This is the mid-cap engine of the portfolio without the unpredictability of an active stock-picker.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: The one active mid-cap fund in the plan — included because its 5-year track record (22.2% CAGR) and 3-year record (26.6% CAGR) are the strongest in its category, even after the 1.15% yearly fee. Nippon is a disciplined, research-driven house with ₹8,172 Cr in this fund. This sits alongside the passive mid-cap index fund: if active management adds value, you benefit; if it doesn't over time, you can shift entirely to the index fund at your annual review. Think of it as an evidence-based experiment with a proven track record.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Small companies (ranked below 250 by market size) carry higher short-term risk — they can drop 40-50% in bad markets — but over 20 years they historically deliver the highest returns. This fund has a 5-year CAGR of 22.3% and manages ₹20,288 Cr, making it one of the largest and most liquid small-cap funds in India. Nippon's consistent long-term outperformance makes it the right pick here. Given your 20-year horizon, short-term pain is easily absorbed. This allocation is capped at 15% to keep overall risk balanced.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: PPFAS is a rare breed in Indian fund management — a value-focused, low-turnover fund that also invests 25-30% in global stocks like Alphabet and Meta. This provides genuine international diversification (your portfolio is otherwise 100% India). At ₹88,403 Cr AUM it is the largest flexi-cap fund in India — a vote of confidence from millions of investors. The 1% yearly fee is justified by the global exposure and the fund house's exceptional governance. This is your geopolitical hedge — if India underperforms in any stretch, your PPFAS holding partially cushions the blow.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A passive index fund tracking the 500 largest US companies — Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Nvidia and 496 others — all in one fund at just 0.20% yearly fee. This is pure international diversification through the most researched, liquid market in the world. 3-year CAGR of 23.8% includes a USD appreciation bonus (rupee depreciation adds to your returns). For the education corpus goal 10 years out (likely in USD or GBP), having a USD-linked asset directly hedges your currency risk. No active manager needed — the S&P 500 index IS the world's most studied benchmark.

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1 — Open a Direct Mutual Fund account (if you haven't already)**
Use Kuvera (kuvera.in) or MFU (mfuindia.com) — both allow you to invest in direct plans (the cheaper version of every fund) without paying a distributor. Groww and Zerodha Coin also work. Ensure KYC is complete (Aadhaar + PAN).

**Step 2 — Park your ₹30 lakh in a liquid fund first**
Move ₹30 lakh to any overnight/liquid fund (e.g., ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund — AMFI 145536) immediately. This earns ~5.5% while you set up the plan and avoids the risk of a lumpsum hit on day one.

**Step 3 — Deploy the first ₹15 lakh lumpsum immediately**
₹9 lakh → UTI Nifty Next 50 (AMFI 143341)
₹6 lakh → Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 (AMFI 147622)

**Step 4 — Set up an STP for the remaining ₹15 lakh**
Set up a Systematic Transfer Plan from your liquid fund: ₹2.5 lakh per month for 6 months, split proportionally across the same two funds. This averages your entry price.

**Step 5 — Set up all 6 monthly SIPs on one date (e.g., 5th of every month)**
₹36,000 → UTI Nifty Next 50 (AMFI 143341)
₹24,000 → Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 (AMFI 147622)
₹18,000 → Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund (AMFI 118668)
₹18,000 → Nippon India Small Cap Fund (AMFI 118778)
₹12,000 → Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund (AMFI 122639)
₹12,000 → Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund (AMFI 148381)
Total: ₹1,20,000/month

**Step 6 — Step up your SIPs every April by 10%**
As your consulting income grows, increase every SIP by 10% each year. This single habit is responsible for a disproportionate share of long-term wealth creation.

**Step 7 — Create a separate education corpus tracker**
Label your S&P 500 + PPFAS holdings mentally as the "education fund." At Year 8, begin gradually moving these two funds' gains into a safer debt fund to protect the corpus from a market crash right before you need it.
review_checkpoints: Year 1 — First annual review: Check if all SIPs are running without interruption. Compare each fund's 1-year return to its category average. If any fund has underperformed its benchmark by more than 5%, note it — but don't act yet. One bad year proves nothing.

Year 3 — Mid-term evidence check: This is the first meaningful data point. Evaluate the two active funds (Nippon Mid Cap and Nippon Small Cap) against their index equivalents. If the active funds have NOT beaten the index fund equivalents after accounting for fees, consider shifting those allocations to the Motilal Nifty Midcap 150 and Motilal Nifty Smallcap 250 index funds. Evidence first, always.

Year 5 — Portfolio rebalancing and goal check: Verify your corpus trajectory against the ₹8 Cr retirement target and education fund target. If equity has grown significantly over your 85% target, trim back to target allocation by moving excess into the S&P 500 fund (more international = more diversified). Also re-evaluate the SIP step-up discipline.

Year 8 — Education fund de-risking begins: Start shifting 20% of your PPFAS + S&P 500 holdings into a short-duration debt fund every 6 months. By Year 10, you want 60-70% of the education corpus in safer instruments. Do not wait for a 'good time to exit' — just execute systematically.

Year 10 — Education corpus crystallisation: The foreign university fund should now be largely in stable assets. Liquidate as needed for fees. Do not disturb the retirement portfolio at this stage — it still has 7 more years of compounding.

Year 15 — Retirement de-risking begins: Begin gradually shifting from small-cap and mid-cap to large-cap index and debt. Target allocation at Year 17 (retirement): 50% equity, 30% debt, 20% international. Start this shift 2 years before retirement, not the week before.
plan.rationale:
Sherlock, here's the thesis: you're 38, want to retire at 55, and need ₹8 Cr in 17 years while also building a foreign education fund in 10 years. Your ₹30 lakh existing corpus and ₹1.2 lakh/month SIP capacity are serious ammunition. The plan is built on three evidence-based pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Index funds are the bedrock (60% of the plan):** At a 0.20% average yearly fee, your index holdings (UTI Nifty Next 50 + Motilal Midcap 150 + Motilal S&P 500) cost roughly ₹240 per ₹1 lakh invested per year. The average active fund charges ₹1,000–1,500. Over 20 years, that 1% fee difference compounding on a ₹3+ Cr corpus costs you ₹1.5–2 Cr in lost wealth. The data is unambiguous.

**Pillar 2 — Selective active exposure where the evidence supports it (30%):** Nippon India Mid Cap and Nippon India Small Cap have delivered 5-year CAGRs of 22%+, consistently outperforming their benchmarks even after fees. These are not speculative bets — they are funds with decade-long evidence trails that you, as a data-driven investor, can track and evaluate. The thesis is reviewed annually.

**Pillar 3 — Global diversification via PPFAS + S&P 500 (20%):** India is 3% of the global economy. Putting 100% in India is a concentration risk. PPFAS + Motilal S&P 500 give you ~20% global exposure at low cost, and the S&P 500 allocation directly hedges the USD cost of your daughter's foreign education.

**Lumpsum deployment:** Your ₹30 lakh corpus should be deployed 50% immediately (₹15 lakh split across UTI Nifty Next 50 and Motilal Midcap 150) and the remaining ₹15 lakh via a 6-month Systematic Transfer Plan (STP) from a liquid fund — this avoids the risk of investing a large amount right before a market correction.

**Net liability position:** You have ₹10 lakh in liabilities. With ₹30 lakh corpus, your net worth is ₹20 lakh. Ensure liabilities are being serviced from income, not from this investment portfolio.

**Retirement math check:** ₹1.2 lakh/month SIP growing at 10% annually, invested over 17 years in a portfolio targeting ~13% CAGR, projects to approximately ₹12–15 Cr — well above your ₹8 Cr target. Even in a conservative scenario (9% CAGR), the corpus exceeds ₹8 Cr. Your goal is achievable with discipline.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.72):** Four of the six funds are explicit index/passive instruments — UTI Nifty Next 50 (30%), Motilal Nifty Midcap 150 (20%), and Motilal S&P 500 (10%) are pure index funds. PPFAS Flexi Cap (10%) is an actively managed fund but with a value/low-turnover style. Nippon Mid Cap (15%) and Nippon Small Cap (15%) are fully active. That gives roughly 60% in pure passive index funds, with the remaining 40% in active or semi-active funds. Weighted fraction ≈ 0.60–0.72 depending on how PPFAS is classified (partially credited given its low-turnover, benchmark-agnostic style).

**turnover_score (0.78):** The plan strongly favours a buy-and-hold SIP approach with annual review checkpoints (Year 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15). Rebalancing is infrequent and systematic rather than tactical. The STP deployment is a one-time structured entry, not ongoing tactical trading. The guidance explicitly says "one bad year proves nothing" and defers action. The de-risking shifts (Year 8, 15) are long-dated and gradual. This is largely a patient, low-turnover framework.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.82):** Cost minimisation is a central, explicitly articulated thesis. The plan quantifies the long-term cost drag of active vs. passive (0.20% vs. 1.0–1.5%), calculates a ₹1.5–2 Cr wealth loss from fee drag over 20 years, and anchors fund selection heavily on expense ratios. The rationale for each index fund prominently features the 0.20% fee. Active funds are included only where track records justify the cost premium, and even then are subject to evidence-based review.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.75):** The plan relies primarily on index-level market exposure for the bulk of the portfolio, with no individual stock analysis. For the active funds (Nippon Mid Cap, Nippon Small Cap, PPFAS), selection is based on aggregate return track records and AUM scale rather than deep company-level or sector-level research. There is no mention of PEG ratios, moat analysis, or manager due diligence beyond fund house reputation. This is closer to broad market exposure with light-touch fund selection screening.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.88):** The plan is explicitly structured around a 17-year retirement horizon and a 10-year education corpus goal. SIP step-ups, de-risking timelines, and review checkpoints are all calibrated to decade-scale compounding. The language consistently discourages short-term reaction ("one bad year proves nothing"). The only partial deduction is for the Year 3 active-vs-passive evidence check and Year 8 de-risking, which introduce some medium-term decision nodes.

**portfolio_activeness_score (0.65):** Risk metrics (beta, alpha, tracking error, information ratio) are absent for all six funds, requiring inference from category and rationale. The three pure index funds (60% allocation) would have near-zero tracking error, beta ≈ 1.0, and alpha ≈ 0 — highly passive. The two Nippon active funds (30%) are mid/small-cap active strategies with likely meaningful tracking error and positive historical alpha (22–27% 3-year CAGRs vs. benchmarks). PPFAS (10%) is a genuinely differentiated fund with global allocation and value tilt, suggesting moderate tracking error. On a weighted basis, the portfolio leans meaningfully passive but carries genuine active conviction in the 30% active sleeve.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.92):** The plan is strongly tailored to Sherlock's profile. The 17-year retirement horizon (retire at 55) is explicitly modelled, with a retirement math check projecting ₹12–15 Cr against the ₹8 Cr target. The 10-year education goal is addressed through designated USD-linked assets (S&P 500 + PPFAS) as a currency hedge, with a concrete de-risking roadmap starting at Year 8. The aggressive growth mandate is served by an 85%+ equity allocation with a meaningful mid/small-cap tilt. The monthly SIP of ₹1,20,000 is fully allocated. The plan also references the net liability position (₹10L liabilities vs. ₹30L corpus). The slight deduction reflects that the retirement horizon is occasionally stated as 17 years (to age 55) vs. the 20-year investment horizon — a minor inconsistency — and projected return figures are narrative rather than formally tabulated.

**Diversification (0.82):** The plan covers large-cap adjacent (Nifty Next 50), mid-cap (passive + active), small-cap (active), flexi-cap with global overlay (PPFAS), and direct US equity (S&P 500) — a solid spread across market-cap segments and geographies. Three fund houses are used (UTI, Motilal Oswal, Nippon, PPFAS), reducing fund-house concentration risk. International exposure is ~20% (PPFAS + S&P 500), which is meaningful. However, the plan is entirely equity with no dedicated debt allocation whatsoever until the de-risking phase begins at Year 8 for the education corpus and Year 15 for retirement — leaving a 30L existing corpus 100% in equities immediately. There is no large-cap Nifty 50 or large-cap blend exposure, meaning the portfolio skews meaningfully toward mid/small risk segments. A debt or hybrid buffer, even small, would improve diversification quality.

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.85):** The aggressive equity-heavy (100%) allocation is consistent with the stated aggressive risk appetite and 20-year horizon. The small-cap warning (40–50% drawdown risk) is explicitly acknowledged in the rationale. Return projections (13% CAGR base, 9% CAGR conservative) are broadly realistic for a diversified India+US equity portfolio over a long horizon. The STP deployment strategy sensibly mitigates lumpsum timing risk. However, the `risks` array is entirely empty — a significant gap for an investor who "expects clear rationale for every recommendation." Downside scenarios beyond the small-cap mention are not formally articulated (e.g., prolonged bear market impact on education corpus, currency risk on S&P 500 returns, rupee depreciation double-edged nature). The 1-year S&P 500 return of 34% is highlighted positively without noting it partially reflects an unusually strong USD appreciation period.

**Internal Consistency (0.87):** The plan is largely coherent. Allocations sum to exactly 100% and SIP amounts sum to ₹1,20,000. The lumpsum deployment (₹9L + ₹6L = ₹15L) matches the stated 50% immediate deployment. The "evidence-based experiment" framing for the active funds aligns well with the investor's data-driven persona and is backed by the Year 3 review checkpoint. The education corpus de-risking roadmap is logically sequenced. Minor inconsistencies: (1) the investment horizon is stated as 20 years in the profile but retirement is at 55 (17 years away) — the plan uses both numbers without reconciling them; (2) `rebalancing_guidelines` is empty despite rebalancing being referenced in review checkpoints; (3) `projected_returns` is empty despite a narrative math check in the rationale; (4) the rationale says "30% active exposure" but Nippon Mid Cap (15%) + Nippon Small Cap (15%) = 30% active, while PPFAS (10%) is also active, making it 40% active — the pillar framing is slightly off.
